perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, either Party possesses certain confidential proprietary information; and WHEREAS, in connection with the pursuit, evaluation and/or feasibility of a business relationship, and/or the consummation of a transaction (collectively, the "Business Purposes") between the two parties hereto, including their affiliates, subsidiaries, stockholders, partners, co-venture, trading partners, employees and other organizations (hereinafter referred to as Affiliates), confidential proprietary information of one Party may become available to the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, either Party possesses certain confidential proprietary information; and WHEREAS, in connection with the pursuit, evaluation and/or feasibility of a business relationship, and/or the consummation of a transaction (collectively, the "Business Purposes") between the two parties hereto, including their affiliates, subsidiaries, stockholders, partners, co-venture, trading partners, employees and other organizations (hereinafter referred to as Service Providers), confidential proprietary information of one Party may become available to the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Affiliates' to 'Service Providers' creates ambiguity. While the original term is broad, replacing it introduces a conflict, especially if the context involves legally defined affiliate relationships. This could affect liability and obligations if 'Service Providers' are interpreted differently under relevant laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The term 'affiliate' has specific legal connotations in corporate law concerning control and relationships between entities. Replacing it with 'Service Providers' can create a loophole because service providers might not meet the legal definition of an affiliate, thus altering the scope of the NDA's protection.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Affiliates' with 'Service Providers', which creates a misaligned terminology issue with potential legal ramifications. The Law Explanation states that 'affiliate' has specific legal connotations in corporate law, while 'Service Providers' might not meet the legal definition of an affiliate. This alteration could narrow the scope of the NDA's protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'affiliate' in 17 CFR § 240.12b-2, highlighting the importance of precise terminology in legal contexts. Replacing 'Affiliates' with 'Service Providers' deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable and creating a legal contradiction, fitting the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information shall mean all strategic and development plans, financial condition, business plans, co-developer identities, data, business records, customer lists, project records, market reports, employee lists and business manuals, policies and procedures, information relating to processes, technologies or theory and all other information which may be disclosed by one Party or to which the other Party may be provided access by the disclosing Party or others in accordance with this Agreement, or which is generated as a result of , incidental to or in connection with the Business Purposes, which is not generally available to the public.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, Confidential Information shall mean all strategic and development plans, financial condition, business plans, co-developer identities, data, business records, customer lists, project records, market reports, employee lists and business manuals, policies and procedures, information relating to processes, technologies or theory and all other information which may be disclosed by one Party or to which the other Party may be provided access by the disclosing Party or others in accordance with this Agreement, or which is generated as a result of , incidental to or in connection with the Business Purposes, regardless of its availability to the public.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'not generally available to the public' clause contradicts the standard definition of confidential information. Publicly available information is typically not protected under NDAs. This modification creates uncertainty about what is actually protected.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret Definition (Uniform Trade Secrets Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret laws, like the UTSA, typically require that information be 'not generally known' to be protected. By including information 'regardless of its availability to the public,' the contract attempts to protect information that does not qualify as a trade secret, potentially rendering this clause unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts standard trade secret and confidential information definitions by including information regardless of its public availability. The Perturbation Explanation notes this contradicts the standard definition of confidential information, as publicly available information is typically not protected under NDAs. The Law Explanation states that trade secret laws, like the UTSA, typically require that information be 'not generally known' to be protected. The change removes this requirement, thus the modified text contradicts the standard definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it will not, for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, initiate contact with the other Party's employees in order to solicit, entice or induce any employee of the other Party to terminate an employment relationship with the other Party to accept employment with the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it will not, for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, initiate contact with the other Party's employees in order to discuss potential business ventures.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause prohibits solicitation of employees. The modified clause prohibits merely discussing potential business ventures. This is overly broad and likely unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. A clause that broadly restricts contact with employees to discuss 'potential business ventures,' even without direct solicitation, could be interpreted as an unlawful restraint of trade, especially if it hinders an employee's ability to pursue independent business opportunities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the restriction from soliciting employees to simply discussing 'potential business ventures.' According to the Law Explanation, this broad restriction on contact, even without direct solicitation, could be interpreted as an unlawful restraint of trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By restricting discussions of 'potential business ventures,' the modified contract potentially restrains employees from engaging in their lawful profession or business, thus creating a legal contradiction and making it likely unenforceable under California law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India and the Party’s country laws on equal force. If there is any conflict of laws, the law of India shall prevail.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the principles of international comity, as determined solely by Party A.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing specific governing law with 'principles of international comity' and granting Party A sole discretion is problematic. International comity is a courtesy, not a defined legal framework, and ceding unilateral control over its interpretation is unfair and likely unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles - Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law typically requires a reasonable relationship between the chosen law and the contract or parties involved. Granting one party the unilateral right to determine the applicable 'principles of international comity' effectively eliminates any objective standard for governing law, contradicting established choice-of-law principles and potentially rendering the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the specified governing law ('laws of India') with 'principles of international comity' as determined solely by Party A. This contradicts established choice-of-law principles. Snippet 1 defines conflict of laws as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute.' The changed text introduces ambiguity and unilateral control, undermining the objective legal standard required for governing law, creating an unenforceable agreement.
